text: Spleen fractures are traumatic injuries which most frequently result from a close trauma following a direct shock in the left thoraco-abdominal region. One should be most wary of spleen fractures when, in addition to the shock, there is a muscle guard of the left hypochondrium, a clinical suspicion of rib fracture to either ribs nine, ten, or eleven, the presence of a care sign on the left side that is an ascending scapular pain, signs of hemorrhagic shock, drop in blood pressure, increase of heart rate, metabolic acidosis. Hemoperitoneum must be actively looked for by echography. Focused assessment sonography for trauma, FAST for short, is designed to achieve this goal. The spleen is an abdominal lymphoid organ located in the left hypochondrium. It may be concerned by every thoraco-abdominal trauma, especially those involving the ninth, tenth, and eleventh ribs. Its functions are the introduction of antigens and the filtering of aging blood cells. It is in contact with other organs, to the front, the posterior side of the stomach, by the gastrolenal ligament, and with the coda pancreatis via the splenopancreatic ligament, above the left diaphragm. behind the upper pole of the left kidney, and it is linked to the left colon flexure by the splenocolic ligament. The splenic vascular anatomy includes the splenic artery, the short gastric vessels which begin at the gastro-mental artery, or which are terminal ramifications of the splenic artery, the inferior and superior polar arteries, the splenic vein on the posterior aspect of the pancreas.

Sterile drapes expose the patient's trunk from the sternal manubrium to the pubic symphysis. The surgeon performs an exifopubic median laparotomy to access the abdominal cavity, preparing to control any lesions. Visible equipment includes air pulsated heating blankets, a scalpel, retractors, aortic clamps for the splenic hilum, blood products, a cell saver, surgical suctions, and swabs.

The surgeon begins by eviscerating the small bowel to rapidly expose the abdominal cavity. A reniform bowl is used to scoop out the hemoperitoneum, which is set aside for treatment and reinfusion. The surgeon then performs general primary packing of the abdominal cavity, starting with the most critical bleeder and continuing in a clockwise manner through the left hypochondrium, right hypochondrium, left lumbar, left iliac fossa, pelvis, right iliac fossa, right lumbar, and umbilical regions.

The surgeon removes the primary packing from the abdominal cavity, starting with the least damaged region, to assess injuries and control hemorrhage. The removal of the packing and mobilization of viscera temporarily reactivate bleeding, prompting the anesthetic team to stabilize the patient. The surgeon prepares for hemostatic surgery, focusing on the critical region in the left hypochondrium.

The surgeon exposes the spleen and carefully clamps the splenic hilum to avoid damaging the caudopancreatus. The splenocolic ligament is divided, providing access to the posterior side of the spleen. The short gastric vessels are clamped, ligated, and divided in stages, allowing for the ligation and sectioning of the splenic hilum.

The surgeon examines the complete abdominal cavity, including the omental bursa, systematically searching for any lesions. The small bowel, colon, retroperitoneum, and diaphragm are inspected. A Radon's drain is placed in the left hypochondrium near the caudal pancreas to manage potential iatrogenic fistulas. The cavity is cleansed thoroughly with physiological serum at 40 degrees Celsius.

The latter can be responsible for inflammation and secondary tissue lesions. Depending on the stability of the patient, the abdomen is either stitched or left open. Splenectomy is an emergency procedure. It must be performed with both speed and respect for the principles that have been described during this presentation. Did you get it?
